name: Anthurium vittariifolium 'Variegated'
origin: Tropical South America; variegated collector form in cultivation
aboutText: Anthurium vittariifolium 'Variegated' is an extraordinary epiphytic aroid native to tropical South America. Recognized for its extremely long, narrow, strap-like pendant leaves, it grows hanging from tree branches. The leathery green foliage showcases beautiful cream-to-yellow marbling and sectors running along the length of the blades. It requires high humidity, consistent moisture, and filtered light to drape elegantly without rot.

morphology.variegation: Creamy white to yellow sectors and marbling
morphology.growthHabit: Hanging habit supported by aerial roots

propagation.notes: Strap-leaf anthuriums rarely branch and are slow to produce offshoots. Stem cuttings require high humidity and warmth. Success rates are lower than velvet types.

careGuide.substrate: Very coarse epiphytic mix: 50% orchid bark, 30% perlite, 20% sphagnum moss. Can also be grown mounted on cork bark for best airflow.
careGuide.watering: Allow to approach dryness between waterings — strap-leaf anthuriums are more drought-tolerant than velvet types. Water thoroughly, then allow to drain completely.
careGuide.humidity: 65–80%. More tolerant of lower humidity than velvet types but still benefit from consistent moisture in the air.
careGuide.fertilising: Half-strength orchid fertiliser monthly during growing season. These are slow growers — overfeeding can burn roots.
careGuide.repotting: Infrequently — every 2–3 years. These species often bloom better when slightly root-bound. Consider mounting on cork board instead.

commonProblems.fix: Switch to a very coarse bark-heavy mix; allow to dry more between waterings
Ensure minimum 18°C and bright indirect light

commonProblems.cause: Low humidity or salt buildup from fertiliser
commonProblems.fix: Flush substrate with clean water to remove salt buildup; increase humidity

items: Count the healthy leaves — strap-leaf anthuriums grow very slowly, so buying a plant with only one leaf means a long wait before it becomes an attractive specimen
Check for scale insects along the underside midrib of leaves — they are small, brown, and flat and easy to miss
Roots should be firm — check that drainage holes are visible in listing photos; root rot from waterlogged shipping medium is common
Ask the seller how many roots the plant has — a well-rooted plant establishes far faster than a recently divided offset

fieldNotes.title: The Hanging Green Ribbons
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Anthurium vittariifolium is a remarkable epiphyte that grows hanging from tree branches in tropical South America. Its leaves can grow up to two meters long while remaining only a few centimeters wide. The variegated cultivar displays stunning streaks and sectors of white and yellow. The pendant habit is an adaptation to shed rain rapidly and avoid collecting debris, which could cause rot. The variegated ribbon-like leaves require a hanging basket or mount to drape gracefully in high humidity.
